title: Assurance Issue Trending Over Time
dataSources: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:issue` from the `issue` modular input in `TA_cisco_catalyst` (Splunkbase 7538). Same data feed as UC-5.13.21. Key distinction: this UC uses `dc(issueId)` per time bucket (unique issues) rather than raw `count` (event count), giving a true measure of how many distinct problems exist at each point in time.
spl:
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue"
| timechart span=4h dc(issueId) as unique_issues by priority
description: Tracks the volume and priority distribution of Assurance issues over time, showing whether the backlog is growing, shrinking, or oscillating — and whether P1/P2 spikes correlate with changes, maintenance windows, or recurring infrastructure problems that haven't been properly root-caused.
value: UC-5.13.21 shows today's backlog. This UC shows the *story* — are there more issues this week than last? Did that firmware push reduce P2 issues? Is the same 'AP Unreachable' issue recurring every Tuesday? The trend line answers the three questions your operations manager asks in every weekly meeting: (1) Is it getting better or worse? (2) What caused that spike? (3) Is our remediation effort working? Since Catalyst Center's own Assurance window is 7 days, Splunk provides the only long-term issue trend for quarterly reviews and year-over-year comparison.
implementation: Same `issue` input as UC-5.13.21. No additional configuration. Use `dc(issueId)` not `count` for unique issue trending. Place below UC-5.13.21's summary table on the Issue Triage dashboard.
visualization: (1) Stacked area chart: `unique_issues` by `priority` over time (P1 red, P2 orange, P3 yellow, P4 grey). (2) Stat panel: total unique issues today vs 7-day average vs 30-day average — showing improvement or degradation. (3) Change-window annotations from `catalyst_maintenance_windows` or audit log events. (4) Optional week-over-week overlay for executive comparison.

references.title: Catalyst Center Intent API — Issues endpoint

knownFalsePositives:
**Issue re-counting across poll cycles inflating trend volume.** If using raw `count` instead of `dc(issueId)`, the same active issue is counted in every poll, making the trend appear flat-high even when the actual backlog is small. Distinguish by comparing `count` vs `dc(issueId)` — if `count >> dc(issueId)`, the inflation is the explanation. This UC's default SPL uses `dc(issueId)` which avoids this.

**P4 informational issues dominating the chart.** P4 issues (sync status, recommendations) can outnumber P1–P3 combined, making the stacked area appear alarming when the operational issues are stable. Distinguish by hiding P4 in the chart or showing it as a muted colour. Suppress with `| where priority IN ("P1","P2","P3")` for operational dashboards.

**Catalyst Center upgrade adding new issue detection categories.** After an upgrade, Assurance may detect new issue types it previously didn't, creating an apparent spike in the trend. Distinguish by checking `| stats earliest(_time) as first_seen by name` for issue names that appeared coincident with the upgrade. Annotate the trend chart with the upgrade date.

**Issue backlog growing during holidays.** If no one resolves issues during a holiday period, the backlog grows — not because more issues are appearing, but because fewer are being closed. Distinguish by checking `| timechart dc(eval(if(status="RESOLVED",issueId,null()))) as resolved` alongside the open count.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• UC-5.13.21 (Issue Summary) must be operational — same `issue` data feed.
• Retain **30–90+ days** of issue data for meaningful trending. The `issue` sourcetype scales with active issue count (~1.7 MB/day for 30 active issues), so long retention is affordable.
• Agree with operations on the trending granularity: `span=4h` for weekly views (balanced detail), `span=1d` for monthly/quarterly views (cleaner lines), `span=1h` for incident investigation (high detail).

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Same `issue` input as UC-5.13.21. No additional configuration.

Confirm sufficient history:
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue" earliest=-30d
| stats earliest(_time) as first latest(_time) as last dc(issueId) as total_issues
| eval days=round((last-first)/86400,1)
| table first, last, days, total_issues
```

Step 2 — Create the search and dashboard panel
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue"
| timechart span=4h dc(issueId) as unique_issues by priority
```

Why `dc(issueId)` not `count`: the `issue` API returns every active issue on each poll. `count` gives event count (= active_issues × polls_in_bucket), which inflates with poll frequency. `dc(issueId)` gives the actual number of unique issues observed in each time bucket — the operationally meaningful metric.

Why `span=4h`: balances detail (enough resolution to see spikes) with readability (not too noisy for 7–30 day views). For incident investigation, narrow to `span=1h`. For executive quarterly views, widen to `span=1d`.

Why `by priority`: shows the mix of P1–P4 issues over time. A growing P1 count is very different from a growing P4 count — the stacked area makes this immediately visible.

For active-only trending (excluding resolved issues the TA might still report):
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue" status!="RESOLVED"
| timechart span=4h dc(issueId) as active_issues by priority
```

For issue resolution rate overlay:
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue"
| timechart span=1d dc(issueId) as total dc(eval(if(status="RESOLVED",issueId,null()))) as resolved
| eval resolution_rate=round(resolved*100/total,1)
```

This is a dashboard panel, not an alert. Schedule weekly for the operations review.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Run the search over the last 7 days. The stacked area should show a recognisable pattern: typically higher during business hours, lower on weekends (when fewer issues are detected because fewer clients are connected and generating problems).

(b) Compare the average daily `unique_issues` count with the count shown in **Catalyst Center > Assurance > Issues**. They should be in the same range (though Catalyst Center shows the current snapshot, not a historical average).

(c) Check for a known incident: if your team responded to a network event last week, there should be a visible spike in the chart at that time.

(d) Verify `dc(issueId)` is materially different from `count`: run `| stats count, dc(issueId) as unique` over the same window. If `count ≈ unique`, the TA may be de-duplicating already. If `count >> unique`, the dc() is essential.

(e) Check that all priority values appear: `| stats dc(issueId) by priority`. If P1 is always 0, that's good (no critical issues) — but verify with the Catalyst Center UI.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard placement (on the "Issue Triage" dashboard, below UC-5.13.21's summary table):
• Full-width stacked area chart: `unique_issues` by priority over the selected time range.
• Time-picker presets: "Last 24 hours" (incident), "Last 7 days" (weekly ops), "Last 30 days" (monthly review).
• Colour coding: P1 red, P2 orange, P3 yellow, P4 grey.
• Annotations: overlay maintenance windows and change events so viewers can correlate spikes with known activity.

Interpretation guide:
• **Steady decline**: remediation is working — the team is resolving more issues than are appearing.
• **Steady incline**: the network is degrading or Assurance is detecting more issues (possibly after an upgrade). Investigate root causes.
• **Spike that recovers**: transient event (maintenance, change). Check the audit log for the corresponding change.
• **Flat line at high volume**: persistent backlog of unresolved issues. These need Problem Management attention, not more alerting.
• **P1/P2 growing while P3/P4 are flat**: the high-priority issues are genuine new problems, not just reclassified P4s.

Weekly review (operations meeting):
• Compare this week vs last week: `| timechart span=1d dc(issueId) | eval week=if(_time>relative_time(now(),"-7d@d"),"this","last")`.
• Ask: did the total issue count go up or down? Did the P1/P2 share change? Did any new issue names appear (UC-5.13.21)?

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Chart shows a flat high line** — you're using `count` instead of `dc(issueId)`. The same issues are being counted on every poll. Switch to `dc(issueId)`.

• **Chart shows zero issues for extended periods** — the `issue` input stopped running. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd "TA_cisco_catalyst" ERROR` for the gap period. Or the network genuinely had zero active issues (rare but possible).

• **Spike to very high numbers after TA or Catalyst Center upgrade** — the API may have returned historical/resolved issues on the first poll after the upgrade. Narrow to `status!="RESOLVED"` if the TA doesn't filter resolved issues.

• **P4 dominates the chart making other priorities invisible** — filter P4 out for the operational view: `| where priority IN ("P1","P2","P3")`. Show P4 in a separate informational chart.

• **Issue count in Splunk doesn't match Catalyst Center** — the GUI shows the *current* snapshot; this timechart shows *historical* unique issues per time bucket. They measure different things. The timechart value for the most recent bucket should approximate the GUI count.

• **Resolution rate appears negative or > 100%** — if using the resolution rate overlay, ensure the resolved count uses the same `issueId` field. A resolved issue that was opened in a previous time bucket creates a mismatch.

• **Chart shows different patterns for different time ranges** — expected. The `span=4h` with a 7-day view shows 42 data points. The same `span=4h` with a 30-day view shows 180 data points. The pattern shape may change as you zoom out.

• **Stale data during weekends** — if the TA runs but no new issues appear (weekends), the chart shows flat lines, not gaps. This is correct — it represents stable issue counts, not missing data.
grandmaExplanation: We draw a chart showing how many network problems exist over time — day by day, week by week. When the number goes up, things are getting worse. When it goes down, fixes are working. Spikes in the chart usually mean something changed that broke things, and the chart helps your team find what that was.
